title: Strain-Enhanced Hydrogen Evolution, Electrical, Optical, and Thermoelectric Properties of the Multifunctional 2D CrSi2N4 Monolayer
authors: Rao Uzair Ahmad, Fahd Sikandar Khan, Nasir Javed
abstract: First-principles density functional theory (DFT) is employed to evaluate the structural, electronic, optical, thermoelectric, and electrocatalytic properties of monolayer CrSi2N4. Its symmetric N-Si-N-Cr-N-Si-N septuple-layer structure exhibits dynamic, thermal (300 K), and mechanical stability, supported by a -8.76 eV/atom cohesive energy. PBE and HSE06 functionals reveal an indirect bandgap of 0.58 eV and 2.16 eV, respectively, driven by localized Cr-3d and N-2p states. The monolayer features 15.57 static dielectric constant and maximum absorption coefficients of 0.9 X 10^6 cm-1 (visible) and 1.4 X 10^6 cm-1 (deep-UV). Semiclassical Boltzmann calculations predict an outstanding room-temperature n-type thermoelectric power factor of 3.5 x mW/mK2. For hydrogen evolution (HER), the basal plane yields a baseline hydrogen adsorption free energy (ΔGH) of 1.05 eV at the N-site. Applying +5% expansive biaxial strain improves HER kinetics, reducing ΔGH to 0.46 eV. Thus, CrSi2N4 is a resilient, tuneable candidate for waste-heat recovery, photodetectors, and sustainable electrocatalysis.
summary: This paper uses DFT to demonstrate that the 2D CrSi2N4 monolayer is a stable, multifunctional material suitable for waste-heat recovery, photodetectors, and hydrogen production. It specifically shows that applying tensile strain can significantly enhance its efficiency as a catalyst for the hydrogen evolution reaction.
main_problem: The study aims to investigate the multifunctional potential of the 2D CrSi2N4 monolayer for applications in electrocatalysis (HER), thermoelectrics, and optoelectronics, specifically focusing on how strain engineering can tune its properties.
main_result: The CrSi2N4 monolayer is found to be stable and highly multifunctional, exhibiting a high n-type thermoelectric power factor and strong optical absorption; notably, +5% biaxial strain significantly improves HER activity by reducing the hydrogen adsorption free energy from 1.05 eV to 0.46 eV.
method: First-principles Density Functional Theory (DFT) using Quantum Espresso, including PBE and HSE06 functionals, phonon dispersion (DFPT), Ab-initio Molecular Dynamics (AIMD), and semiclassical Boltzmann transport theory.
model_or_system: A 2D CrSi2N4 monolayer with a symmetric septuple-layer (N-Si-N-Cr-N-Si-N) structure belonging to the P-6m2 space group.
key_observables: Cohesive energy, phonon dispersion, bandgap, static dielectric constant, absorption coefficients, Seebeck coefficient, power factor, and hydrogen adsorption free energy (delta GH).
important_parameters: Biaxial strain ranging from -5% to +5%, temperature range from 300 K to 700 K, and relaxation time of 10 fs.
main_assumptions: Constant relaxation time approximation (CRTA) for transport properties and use of the Computational Hydrogen Electrode (CHE) method for electrocatalysis.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows phonon dispersion and AIMD energy evolution; Figure 7 displays the electronic band structure under strain; Figure 8 illustrates the modulation of hydrogen adsorption free energy under strain; Table 4 provides a comparative analysis of catalytic performance against other 2D materials.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from structural and mechanical stability analysis to electronic and optical properties, followed by thermoelectric transport calculations, and concludes with strain-engineered electrocatalytic performance for the hydrogen evolution reaction.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) Top and (b) side views of the optimized 𝑪𝒓𝑺𝒊𝟐𝑵𝟒 septuple-layer monolayer (N-Si- N-Cr-N-Si-N). High-symmetry bond lengths are indicated for the Cr-N (1.98 Å) and Si-N (1.71– 1.74 Å) interfaces.

Figure 2. Dynamic and thermal stability of the 𝑪𝒓𝑺𝒊𝟐𝑵𝟒 monolayer. (a) Phonon dispersion spectra displaying the absence of imaginary frequencies. (b) Ab-initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) evolution of total energy over 10 ps at 300 K.

Figure 3. Electronic properties of monolayer 𝑪𝒓𝑺𝒊𝟐𝑵𝟒. (a, b) Band structures and density of states calculated using GGA-PBE (c) Band structure calculated using HSE06 hybrid functionals, revealing an indirect bandgap of 2.16 eV. (d) Atom-projected density of states (PDOS) highlighting the dominant Cr-3d and N-2p orbital contributions near the Fermi level

Figure 4. Calculated thermoelectric transport coefficients as a function of chemical potential (𝝁 − 𝑬𝑭) from 300 K to 700 K: (a) Seebeck coefficient, (b) electrical conductivity, (c) electronic thermal conductivity, and (d) power factor.

Figure 4c displays the electronic thermal conductivity (𝜅𝑒/𝜏). The structural profile of the 𝜅𝑒 curves is nearly identical to that of the electrical conductivity, a direct manifestation of the Wiedemann-Franz law:
